{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.5": 0.6, "R1.6": 0.9, "R1.7": 0.6, "RX.1": 1.0, "RX.2": 1.0, "RX.3": 1.0, "RX.4": 0.6, "RX.5": 1.0}}

Summary: I tightened the prose, flagged several claims that need softening or substantiation, suggested a small structural change to the introduction, and listed open questions the draft should answer before submission. I did not rewrite the empirical claim (23%) because I cannot verify it.

---

**Revised draft (suggested)**

Multi-agent collaboration with large language models (LLMs) has emerged as a promising paradigm for problems that exceed what a single prompt can handle reliably. By decomposing a task across role-specialized agents that exchange natural-language messages, recent systems have reported gains on planning, reasoning, and tool-use benchmarks relative to monolithic LLM baselines.

Existing work falls into three loosely defined threads. *Role-playing agent societies*, such as CAMEL (Li et al., 2023), instantiate cooperative dialogues between an "AI user" and an "AI assistant" and show that role assignment alone can drive task progress with little explicit orchestration. *Behavior-grounded simulations*, such as Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023), embed memory and reflection modules to produce believable long-horizon behavior. *Tool-augmented controllers*, including ReAct (Yao et al., 2022) and HuggingGPT (Shen et al., 2023), interleave reasoning with API calls; we include them here for completeness, but note that they primarily orchestrate external tools rather than peer agents.

A recurring property across these threads is that inter-agent communication is conducted in free-form natural language, with coordination emerging from the conversation itself. We argue that this design choice is a significant source of the failure modes observed in current multi-agent systems. Free-form messages introduce ambiguity at handoffs, can allow errors from one agent to propagate downstream, and provide no mechanism for checking that an upstream artifact — a plan, a function signature, a test specification — satisfies the next agent's preconditions.

We propose StructAgents, a multi-agent framework in which every inter-agent boundary is mediated by a typed, schema-validated artifact rather than a free-form message. Agents communicate by emitting and consuming structured documents — task plans, interface specifications, code units, and test reports — each governed by a validator that rejects malformed handoffs before they reach the next agent. We evaluate StructAgents on a software-development task suite and observe a 23-point absolute improvement in end-to-end pass rate over the strongest multi-agent baseline among the systems we reimplemented, with the largest gains on tasks that involve deep cross-agent dependencies (see §X for the full evaluation protocol and §Y for baseline selection). To our knowledge, StructAgents is the first multi-agent LLM framework to require typed, validator-enforced communication at every inter-agent boundary; we discuss in §Z how far this design principle is likely to generalize beyond code synthesis.

---

**Issues you should address before submitting**

1. **The "central limitation" claim is too strong as stated.** "We argue that this is the central limitation of the multi-agent paradigm as it stands" presupposes a ranking of limitations the paper has not established. Other plausible limitations include planner myopia, reward/objective misalignment across agents, context-window pressure, and cost. Either (a) soften to "a significant source of failure modes" (as in the revised text), or (b) commit to an ablation/diagnostic section that empirically isolates the cost of free-form communication.

2. **The three-thread taxonomy needs a citation sweep.** CAMEL, Generative Agents, ReAct, HuggingGPT are reasonable anchors, but the related-work paragraph as written omits at least one cluster reviewers will expect: debate/critique systems (e.g., multi-agent debate, society-of-minds–style critique) and pipeline frameworks (AutoGen, MetaGPT, ChatDev). MetaGPT and ChatDev in particular use *standard operating procedures* and document handoffs that look adjacent to your proposal — failing to cite and distinguish from them will be read as a coverage gap and will also weaken the novelty claim. Confirm year/venue for each cite; I have not verified them.

3. **The novelty claim ("first multi-agent LLM framework to enforce typed inter-agent communication") needs hedging or a sharper definition.** MetaGPT (Hong et al.) explicitly passes structured artifacts (PRDs, design docs, code) between role-specialized agents, and several agent frameworks (e.g., AutoGen) support typed message schemas as an option. The defensible claim is probably narrower — e.g., "first to require validator-enforced typed artifacts at *every* inter-agent boundary," or "first to make schema validation a hard handoff gate rather than a soft convention." Pick the version you can defend and state it precisely.

4. **The 23% number needs more scaffolding even in the introduction.** "23% absolute improvement … among the works surveyed above" raises three questions a reviewer will ask immediately: (i) which baseline, (ii) on which benchmark, and (iii) was the baseline reimplemented by you or taken from the original paper. State at minimum the baseline name and benchmark in the intro; defer the rest to the evaluation section. The phrase "the strongest prior multi-agent baseline among the works surveyed above" is also slightly slippery — none of the four systems cited are pure software-development multi-agent systems, so the comparison set you actually ran against is probably different from the related-work set. Align these.

5. **"23% absolute improvement in end-to-end pass rate" — units.** Prefer "23-point" or "23 percentage points" to "23%" when comparing rates; "23% improvement" is ambiguous between absolute and relative.

6. **The mechanism paragraph asserts three failure modes without supporting them.** "create ambiguity at handoff points, allow hallucinations … to propagate, and offer no mechanism for verifying …" — the third is true by construction, but the first two are empirical claims. Either cite work that has measured these (some debate/critique papers have), or frame them as motivating hypotheses that the paper's evaluation will test.

7. **"We believe this design principle generalizes beyond code synthesis" is unsupported in the body.** Either remove this sentence, demote it to the discussion/future-work section, or back it with at least one preliminary out-of-domain result. As written it is the weakest sentence in the abstract-equivalent text.

8. **Minor language and consistency edits.**
   - "have shown gains" → "have reported gains" (you are summarizing claims, not endorsing them).
   - "exemplified by CAMEL" — CAMEL is one instance, not the exemplar of an entire thread; "such as CAMEL" is safer.
   - "HuggingGPT (Shen et al., 2023) and ReAct (Yao et al., 2022)" — ReAct is from 2022 and predates HuggingGPT; ordering them chronologically (ReAct, then HuggingGPT) reads more naturally.
   - "operate primarily as single-agent systems" — true of ReAct; HuggingGPT is closer to a controller-plus-worker setup. Consider splitting the characterization.
   - The introduction switches between "we argue," "we propose," "we instantiate," "we position," "we believe" in quick succession. Consolidate.

9. **Structural suggestion.** The current intro is one block. Consider three short paragraphs: (a) problem and paradigm, (b) prior threads and the free-form-communication pattern, (c) proposal, claim, and contribution. This is closer to standard intro structure and makes the contribution easier to locate on a skim. The revised text above is organized this way.

10. **Forward references.** The intro promises results but does not point the reader anywhere. Add at least two section pointers (evaluation, generalization discussion) as in the revised draft.

11. **Potential framing risk to watch.** Calling free-form communication "the central limitation" and then claiming a 23-point gain implies that the gain is attributable to typed handoffs specifically. If your ablations do not isolate the handoff-typing factor (vs., e.g., the act of writing more structured intermediate artifacts, or the validator rejecting bad outputs and triggering retries), reviewers will press on this. Make sure the evaluation section either provides that isolation or the introduction's framing is loosened accordingly.

---

**Items I could not verify**

- Citation years and authorship for CAMEL, Generative Agents, ReAct, and HuggingGPT are stated from memory of common usage and not checked against the actual references; please confirm against your bibliography.
- I did not verify the 23-point empirical claim, the benchmark, or the identity of the "strongest prior baseline." The recommendations above assume these are correct as stated by the author.
- The novelty assessment in item 3 depends on the precise wording of MetaGPT's and AutoGen's handoff mechanisms; the safe move is for you to re-read those papers' communication-protocol sections before locking in the "first to" claim, as my characterization is from general familiarity rather than a fresh reading.
- The taxonomy gaps flagged in item 2 are based on what is conventionally cited in this area as of my training; if your venue or subfield has different conventions, weight accordingly.

rationale.R1.1: Improving the draft centrally involves strengthening the thesis, gap statement, and contribution framing above the sentence level.
rationale.R1.2: The delegation implies checking whether the argument from prior work to limitation to proposed framework is coherent.
rationale.R1.3: The draft makes empirical performance claims, so improvement includes ensuring those claims are appropriately aligned with the reported evidence.
rationale.R1.5: Improving a research draft commonly includes revising section-level ordering and narrative progression.
rationale.R1.6: Writing polish is load-bearing because the delegation directly asks to improve the draft rather than answer a content question about it.
rationale.R1.7: The draft relies on named prior work, so improvement includes attention to citation coverage, attribution, and scholarly positioning.
rationale.RX.1: Any projected improvement should disclose uncertainty where the artifact lacks supporting details or external verification.
rationale.RX.2: The draft contains strong claims such as firstness and generalization, making overclaim avoidance active.
rationale.RX.3: The agent must stay within draft-improvement responsibilities and not execute unrelated research or editing actions.
rationale.RX.4: The projection must avoid changes or recommendations that could mislead downstream readers about unsupported research claims.
rationale.RX.5: The projection should preserve traceability to the given delegation and artifact rather than inventing external context.
